title: practical english usage - Part 201
content:
Where are the President and his wife staying?

INDIRECT:                                     not She asked what she needed, (     .. .

INDIRECT:                                                     notusually He wanted to know when I was leaving, (                 . . . when was DIRECT: When are you leaving?

INDIRECT:                                  notusually I asked where Alice was. (                 ... where was Alice.) -  DIRECT: Where's Alice?

English, and auxiliary do is not used.

In reported questions the subject normally comes before the verb in standard 1 word    order:    / asked       where       Alice     was.

260 indi  re ct  speech:    ques   tions    and   ans  wers INDIRECT: The government has announced that taxes will be raised.

Taxes will be raised.

I'll tell her you need some help.

We need some help.

He says he doesn't want to play any more.

I don't want to play any more.

same as in the original (because there is no important change of time).

After present, future and present perfect reporting verbs, tenses are usually the 5  No  tense     change      after    He  says.     I'll tell    her,    etc For details. ►    263.2.

Didn't you hear me? I asked how old you are.

in these cases.

However, it is often also possible to keep the original speaker's tenses INDIRECT: Sorry, I didn't realise this was your seat.

That is my seat.

Didn't you hear me? I asked how old you were.

Your mother said I could use her car today. Have you got the keys?

You can use my car today.

I told the police I was British. (The speaker is still British.) -    DIRECT: I'm British.

things the original speaker said are still true.

After past reporting verbs, we  usually change the original tenses even if the 4 / told     them      I was     British.

Noun Clauses, Direct and Indirect Speech    Section 24 grammar »261     whether and if if n> gi*i married. ..) not They can't decide whether to get married now or wait, (     They can't decide Whether, but not if, is used before to-infinitives.

I haven't settled the question of whether I'll go back home.

(     .. .-about if-we should-move ...) There was a big argument about whether we should move to a new house.

After prepositions, only whether is possible.

Whether I'll have time I'm not sure at the moment.

ɪf an indirect question is fronted (►  272), whether is used.

reinvest the profits.

The Directors have not decided whether they will recommend a dividend or In a formal style, whether is usually preferred in a two-part question with or.

We discussed whether we should close the shop. (More normal than After verbs that are more common in a formal style, whether is preferred.

1 asked whether/if she had any messages for me.

I'm not sure whether/if I'll have time.

Whether and if can both introduce indirect questions.

1  indirect      questions 261 whether         and   if For the difference between say and tell, ►    572.

He never says where he's going.  I told her what time it was.

Please say whether you want the town centre.

But say and tell can introduce the answers to questions.

n&druwsaid whether I wanted the town centre; Say and tell are not used to report questions.

4 say    and   tell:   answers,      not   questions not I'm not sure if I'll see her tomorrow, (     ... if I see her tomorrow.) the future.

In reported questions, we  do not use a present tense after if to talk about I don't know if/whether I can help you.

The driver asked if/whether I wanted the town centre.

Yes/No questions are reported with if or whether (for the difference, ►    261).

3   yes/no      questions:      He  asked       if  ...

We asked where the money was. (     .. .-where the money was?) Question marks are not used in reported questions.

2 no   question       marks We're wondering will we get to Cardiff on time, will we be able to park.

He asked when was I leaving.

order as direct questions: ɪn very informal speech, indirect questions sometimes have the same word grammar •  262  indirect speech: infinitives For the structures that are possible after particular verbs, see a good dictionary*.
